attributes.stem: A 40-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a three-week history of painless swelling of the left breast. She has no history of trauma to the breast. She reports a scant amount of bloody discharge from the nipple and has not noticed any fluctuation in the size of the swelling. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She is nulliparous and of African ethnicity. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. The left breast is erythematous and warm, with no discrete palpable mass (Image). Her BMI is 35 kg/m2.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient's presentation is most consistent with inflammatory carcinoma of the breast, a rare type of cancer that tends to occur in younger women (under 50 years) compared to locally advanced breast cancers, and more often in African than Caucasian women. A high BMI is an independent risk factor. Patients rarely present with generalised symptoms e.g. fatigue and weight loss, and there may not be a palpable mass. The irregular dimpling, oedema and 'pin-point' pitting is the result of invasion and obstruction of sub-dermal lymphatics; this clinical picture may be referred to as "peau d'orange" skin.

Cellulitis is an infection of the dermis and subcutaneous tissue in the skin, which presents as a painful, erythematous, and oedematous region of skin. As the infection progresses, the region of erythema spreads and increases in size. The irregular blistering and dimpling of the skin, and the absence of pain in the history is uncharacteristic of cellulitis.

Granulomatous mastitis is a rare, chronic inflammatory condition of the breast. It typically presents as a firm, tender mass that may be associated with skin changes, nipple retraction, and axillary lymphadenopathy. While it can mimic inflammatory breast cancer, it is more commonly seen in younger women with a recent history of pregnancy or lactation, which is not the case for this patient.

Paget's disease of the breast is a red, scaly, eczema-like rash of the nipple/areolar area. This is the superficial manifestation of an underlying ductal carcinoma (in-situ or invasive). Risk factors for a ductal carcinoma include age over 50, personal/family history of breast malignancy, and hormone replacement therapy - none of which are in this patient's history. Lesions may fissure and ooze.

Necrotising fasciitis can present similarly as an erythematous, necrotic skin lesion. However, it is associated with trauma to the skin barrier, significant pain and tenderness, and generalised symptoms of infection (malaise, fatigue, vomiting, diarrhoea). Patients with necrotising fasciitis are often seriously unwell.
learningPoints: Inflammatory breast cancer is a rare type of breast cancer associated with a younger age at diagnosis, high BMI, and African descent.
Inflammatory carcinoma of the breast can present with rapidly enlarging and irregular breast swelling, itching, dimpling (peau d'orange), ulceration and nipple retraction/distortion.

Inflammatory breast cancer can be easily mistaken for mastitis or cellulitis, but often does not present with general signs of infection e.g. fever, malaise, fatigue, and will not improve with antibiotics.
references: Kanitakis, J. Mammary and extramammary Paget's disease. Journal of the European Academy of Dermatology and Venereology [Internet]. 2007 [Accessed 2023 Jan 26];21(5): 581-590. Available from: https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1468-3083.2007.02154.x

Farne H, Norris-Cervetto E, Warbrick-Smith J. Oxford Cases in Medicine and Surgery 2nd Ed [Internet]. Oxford: Oxford University Press, Incorporated; 2016 [Accessed 2023 Jan 26]. Available from: https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/uwa/reader.action?docID=5891951

van Uden, D J P et al. Inflammatory breast cancer: an overview. Critical reviews in oncology/hematology [Internet] 2015 [Accessed 2023 Jan 26];93(2): 116-126. Available from: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.critrevonc.2014.09.003

Mamouch F, Berrada N, Aoullay Z, El Khanoussi B, Errihani H. Inflammatory Breast Cancer: A Literature Review. World J Oncol [Internet]. 2018 [Accessed 2023 Mar 03];9(5-6):129-135. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6279456/

Brennan M, Morgan M, Jeilat G, Kanesalingam K. Granulomatous lobular mastitis: Clinical update and case study. Australian Journal of General Practice. 2020;49(1-2):44-47. Available online: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32008263/
